text: vv. 1-7. The Latin version of David’s canticle disagrees in several places with the Hebrew text, and also with the (widely different) version given in the Septuagint Greek; it seems likely that there was some early corruption in the manuscripts.

‘Jesbaam the son of Hachamoni’; so the Latin version transliterates the name in I Par. 11.11; here, evidently finding the same names slightly distorted in the Hebrew original, it has translated them as if they were common nouns, ‘a very wise man sitting in a chair’. The phrase ‘the same that was called Plump as a Wood-worm’ is an attempt to translate two words of uncertain signification in the Hebrew text, which possibly conceal a fresh proper name. Some think they are a corruption of the words ‘he lifted his spear’ (as in Paralipomena).
‘His cousin’; this may also be read as a proper name, ‘the son of Dodo’. So also in verse 24 below.
‘Two heroes’; the Hebrew word here used, of uncertain significance, is translated as ‘lions’ in the Latin version here, and transliterated as ‘ariels’ in I Par. 11.22.
